policy.phoneme_reason: Pure phonemes require SSP-specialist recording and listening approval.

items.text: Get comfortable. You may look at the listening card, look away, move quietly or use replay.
You do not need to remember every word. Listen for who, what and where.
Who? Mina. What? She splashed. Where? By the gate. Red tells us about the boots, but it is not the place.
Hear the whole part before choosing. Replay if you need it. Check that every part of the picture fits.
Point to the part you heard that helped you work it out.
Put the three pictures in order. You can tell me, point, tap or ask an adult to say your answer.
Listen means get ready to notice the important spoken words.
A detail is one important part of what you heard.
Sequence means the order in which things happen.
A clue is a part you heard that helps you work something out.
Let's wake up the sounds first.
Listen: c...a...t. Now sweep them together: cat.
c, a, t. Sweep: cat.
Tap, tap, tap. Now sweep.
Listen for the middle sound.
Can you help me blend the sounds?
A sound is one small part we can hear in a word.
Blend means sweep the sounds together until they become a word.
Follow the path slowly. Start at the glowing dot.
Letters have a start, a path and a finish.
Start near the top. Curve around like a small moon.
Stay close to the trail. It is okay to try again.
Find the start point before you trace.
I started at the bottom and got muddled. Can you show me the start?
The start point tells your pencil where to begin.
Direction is the path your pencil follows.
Say the whole word once. Now get ready to listen inside it.
The word is sun. Stretch it slowly and listen for each sound.
Say sat. Stretch it. I hear three sounds. One counter goes in each box.
Listen first. Push one counter per sound. Then build the word in the same order.
Stretch the word. Touch each counter. Which sound comes between the first and last?
I heard the first and last sounds. How do I find the sound in the middle?
A word is the whole thing we say before we stretch it.
A phoneme is one sound in a spoken word.
Segment means say the sounds in order, one by one.
Push one counter into a box for each sound.
Ask who or what the phrase is about. Boat is the noun.
Choose details that help the reader picture or identify the noun.
Start with the. Add small and spotted. End with the noun dog. Read the whole phrase to check it sounds right.
Build the phrase. Listen to the sentence. Remove any detail that repeats or does not help.
The phrase names the bike, but it does not yet tell us what the bike does or is.
Tell Pip which detail helps the reader and which words repeat the same idea.
A noun is a naming word. It tells us who or what.
A noun phrase is built around a noun.
An expanded noun phrase tells the reader more about the noun.
An adjective can tell us what a noun is like.
A determiner helps point to which noun we mean.
Listen first. Then we will read together.
Rereading is a strategy strong readers use.
A question mark helps our voice sound like a question.
Group words that belong together.
Good rereading checks every word.
I thought rereading meant I did badly. Is that true?
Fluency means reading smoothly, accurately and with meaning.
Rereading helps your reading grow smoother.
Listen to both whole words. Hold the shared sound in your mind.
Now look closely. Which letters are doing the sound-writing job?
Listen, then check the letters. We learn which spelling belongs in each word.
Happy ends consonant y. Change y to i, then add er: happier.
Use the produced recording. Build left to right and check each part.
How do I listen safely and then check the spelling evidence?
A spelling pattern is a useful letter pattern we can notice and reuse.
A grapheme is how we write a phoneme.
The base word carries the main meaning before a suffix is added.
A suffix is added at the end and can change the word.
Look for the word that opens the sentence gate.
A capital letter opens the sentence. A punctuation mark closes it.
This sentence is asking something, so it needs a question mark.
Build one complete idea from start to end.
Listen to what the sentence is doing.
Can you teach me which end sign I need?
A sentence tells one complete idea.
A question mark shows the sentence asks something.
Find who or what the sentence is about, then find the action or state.
Useful detail can identify, describe or explain. Extra words are not always better.
Because gives a cause. Although gives an unexpected contrast. The link must match the meaning.
Build, read, and check after each addition. Keep the main message easy to find.
Because introduces a reason. Ask what happened because the gate was locked.
Point to each added part, name its job and remove anything that does not help.
A noun phrase names something and can include useful detail.
A clause contains a verb and adds an action, event or state.
A conjunction can link ideas by time, cause, condition or contrast.
Prepositions such as beneath, after and during begin detail phrases.
Precise detail earns its place by helping the reader.
Name the answer type first. It helps you recognise the right evidence.
Choose important words, predict the useful section, then scan and check the full sentence.
Do not stop because one keyword is missing. Read for words with the same or a related meaning.
Check that your highlighted words answer this exact question, then turn them into a clear answer.
Information can be stated in more than one part of a non-fiction page.
Show the exact evidence and explain how it answers the question.
Retrieve means locate information that is stated in the text.
A keyword helps guide your search, but the text may use a related word instead.
Scan by checking headings and moving your eyes or reading focus for useful clues.
Relevant information answers the question; an interesting extra fact may not.
Read the whole sentence, predict the kind of meaning needed, then test a word in the gap.
First predict, then collect clues, test a meaning and reread the sentence.
Pass gives a starting idea, im and able change it, and turning back confirms that the path could not be passed.
Check the word class and numbered meanings, then reread the sentence with your chosen definition.
Explain the whole phrase, then point to the context that supports your explanation.
Say your first prediction, name the strongest clue, test the meaning and reread to check.
Context means the clues around a word and the situation in which it is used.
A clue is evidence, not just a nearby word. It must help the sentence make sense.
A root word can give you a starting meaning, but context still decides what fits.
Choose the definition that matches the word's job and meaning in this sentence.
A synonym can replace the word without changing the important meaning of the sentence.
Sometimes the whole phrase matters, so do not explain each word separately.
A paragraph groups sentences that belong together.
Put notes about the same thing into one paragraph bundle.
Because all three sentences describe the entrance, they belong in one paragraph.
Read or listen for what the sentence is mostly about.
One sentence changes the idea. Find the odd one out.
I made every sentence its own paragraph. Why does that feel jumpy?
A paragraph keeps related sentences together.
The theme is the idea the paragraph is about.
A clear pronoun points back to one person, place or thing.
Sometimes a pronoun is smooth. Sometimes a noun phrase is clearer.
If a pronoun could point to more than one person, use a name or noun phrase to make it clear.
Ask whether the link shows time, cause, order or contrast before choosing it.
Good cohesion is not just fewer repeated words. It must still be clear.
I made it shorter, but now I cannot tell what 'it' means. Can you help me choose?
A pronoun can replace a noun, but it must be clear who or what it means.
A noun phrase can remind the reader exactly which person, place or thing is meant.
Cohesion means the ideas in your writing connect clearly.
Cause language explains why something happened.
Sentences in one paragraph should belong to the same idea island.
A paragraph gathers sentences that tell the reader about one connected idea.
The topic sentence should be broad enough for all the details that follow.
When the focus changes, a new paragraph can help the reader.
Every sentence should connect to the paragraph's main idea.
I pressed enter after every sentence. Can you help me group the ideas?
A paragraph is a group of sentences that belong together around one idea.
The theme is the idea that each sentence in the paragraph should connect to.
A topic sentence tells the reader what this paragraph is mostly about.
Linking words help the reader move from one idea to the next.
Some answers are stated. Other answers are worked out from clues.
The clue leads to an idea, and the idea must still fit the clue.
Pushing the note deep into his pocket is the clue. It suggests a hiding motive because he puts it out of sight.
Choose an idea that fits, then highlight only the words that help prove it.
Strong readers say what the clue supports, not everything that might be possible.
Show me the clue for each idea and explain why both could fit.
An inference is a sensible idea made from clues in the text.
Evidence is the part of the text that makes your inference believable.
A motive is what a character wants or the reason for an action.
A plausible inference fits what the text shows, even if more than one answer is possible.
Some phrases tell us when, where or how something happens.
We can move the detail to the front to guide the reader.
After sunset is the fronted adverbial. The comma comes after the whole phrase.
Put the opener first, add a comma, then finish the main clause.
Find the whole fronted adverbial before placing the comma.
I put the comma after the first word. Can you show me why that is not enough?
A fronted adverbial sits at the front and adds detail about when, where or how.
After the fronted adverbial, add a comma before the main clause.
Notice the exact changed word, then say what it suggests in this sentence.
Start with what is actually written. Explain what it suggests here, then use may, might or encourages for the reader response.
Clawed suggests repeated animal-like attack. This makes the storm seem hostile and may make the station feel vulnerable.
Keep the event the same. Change one word, sentence pattern or viewpoint choice, then compare what each version foregrounds.
A strong explanation can point back to exact language and the situation in the text.
Quote briefly, explain the contextual meaning and say the response the choice may encourage.
An authorial choice is something the writer selects to shape meaning or effect.
Connotations are the extra ideas and feelings a word can carry.
A reader effect is a response the writer's choice may encourage.
Viewpoint controls what the reader sees, knows and notices.
A clear reference has one sensible destination.
Good links show what continues, changes, happens next or moves elsewhere.
This supply points clearly to rainwater. Later that week moves the reader forward in time.
Reread after every edit. A link should make the relationship clearer, not merely make the sentence longer.
If a reference arrow can point to two nouns, repeat the important noun or rewrite the sentence.
Explain whether each signpost shows order, simultaneous time or place.
Cohesion guides the reader from one idea to the next.
A reference word must point clearly to the right person, object or idea.
A cohesive adverbial is a signpost chosen for the relationship between ideas.
Tense choices can keep a time sequence clear or show a deliberate shift.
First find what the text says clearly.
A good inference needs a clue from the text.
The action is the clue. Checking the door again and again can show nervousness.
Choose the smallest useful clue.
The best evidence must prove the idea.
Can you tell me what the clue shows?
An inference is what you work out from clues.
Evidence is the part of the text that supports your answer.
Ask which person, thing, place or time the extra clause describes.
The link may show a person, thing, place, time or possession, and sometimes the pronoun can be implied.
Which points back to the observatory. The clause is inserted after that noun, and the main sentence remains complete around it.
Keep the intended noun close, remove repeated words and check that the main clause still works.
A relative word introduces a clause that describes a noun. Not every that does this job.
Name the antecedent, explain the meaning link and show that the sentence remains clear.
A relative clause points back to a noun and adds information about it.
The antecedent is the person, place, time, thing or possession being described.
Choose the relative word that matches the meaning you need.
Sometimes the linking word can be left out because the relationship stays clear.
Ask who or what performs the action, what the action is, and what is affected.
Precise grammar helps a reader understand exactly what you mean.
The active version foregrounds the committee. The passive version foregrounds the plan.
First decide the intended meaning. Then choose the grammar that expresses it accurately.
A semicolon can link two closely related independent clauses.
Explain how each modal verb changes the strength of the message.
The subject is what the clause is about.
The object receives or is affected by the action.
In active voice, the subject does the action.
In passive voice, the affected person or thing is brought into focus.
Modal verbs show how possible or necessary something is.
An independent clause can make complete sense on its own.
Find a clue in the words, not just in your head.
A strong inference links the clue to your answer.
Hiding the map is evidence. It suggests she may feel guilty or worried.
Pick your inference, then prove it with text evidence.
The best because sentence explains how the clue proves the inference.
I found a quote. Why do I still need to explain it?
An inference is what you work out from clues.
Evidence is the part of the text that proves your thinking.
To justify, explain how the evidence supports your inference.
Some words point back to an earlier noun or idea.
Cohesion is the thread that holds writing together.
It points back to the machine, so the reader knows what shook.
Choose the link that matches the relationship between ideas.
If the reader cannot tell what 'it' means, the writing needs repair.
I linked everything with and then. Why does it sound weak?
Cohesion helps ideas connect so the reader can follow.
A reference word points back to something already mentioned.
Adverbials can help the reader move through ideas.
Writers control effect by choosing structure, words and punctuation deliberately.
Each choice changes how the reader understands the sentence.
For a formal report, choose precise and neutral language: Many visitors left litter across the site.
A dash can create a pause before important or surprising information.
A controlled sentence guides the reader through ideas without making them untangle everything.
Punctuation is useful when it helps meaning. It is not a badge for harder writing.
Writing control means choosing words, sentences and punctuation on purpose.
Register is the level of formality in your writing.
Emphasis helps the reader notice what matters most.
Precision means being exact, not vague.
Tone is how the writing sounds to the reader.
A useful claim takes a clear position that reasons and evidence can support.
A strong argument has a route. Each reason supports the claim, and each piece of evidence supports a reason.
The survey matters because it shows that many pupils need access after the current closing time.
Ask whether the evidence directly supports this reason and whether the reader can trust it.
A rebuttal should answer the real challenge, not dismiss the person making it.
I found three facts about the topic. What must I add so the reader can follow my reasoning?
A claim is the main position your argument will support.
Evidence gives the reader a sound basis for accepting a reason.
A counterargument shows what a thoughtful person on the other side might say.
A rebuttal responds fairly, then explains why the main claim still stands.
Coherence means the reader can follow how each part of the argument connects.
Some words change meaning depending on the subject and sentence around them.
Prefixes, roots and suffixes are clues. They do not replace context, but they give us a strong first prediction.
In science, observe means to notice or measure carefully so evidence can be recorded.
Read the whole sentence. Ask what work the word is doing in this subject.
A useful glossary card shows what the word means, how it is built, where it belongs and how it works in a sentence.
I chose the longest word because it sounded clever. What should I check before using it?
Disciplinary vocabulary means subject words used precisely in that discipline.
Morphology helps us break a word into meaningful parts.
The root is the central meaning part of a word.
Context gives clues from the sentence, paragraph and subject.
Register is the level of language that fits the audience, purpose and subject.
Writers choose words that shape the mood.
A strong literary answer links evidence to an inference and explains the effect.
Swallowed suggests the darkness feels powerful, so the mood may be threatening.
The best quote directly supports the idea.
Look for the answer that explains how the words affect the reader.
I said what happened. Why is that not enough?
Mood is the atmosphere the writing creates for the reader.
A connotation is what a word suggests or makes us feel.
Analysis explains how the evidence proves your idea.
See the group first. Count each object once if you need to.
First show the start. Then show what happens. Last show the amount we need.
Keep six in your head and count on: seven, eight, nine. Six plus three equals nine.
Start with the whole. Move four away once. The cubes still on the mat are left.
Match the rows. The extra unmatched shells show the difference.
Build both sides. If each side has the same amount, the equation is true.
Altogether asks for the whole amount.
Left means the amount that remains.
The difference is the gap between two amounts.
An equation uses numbers and signs to show equal amounts.
The missing number completes the story.
See the whole group first. You can count if you need to.
We can split the seeds in different ways. No seeds appear or disappear.
The parts are two and three. The whole is five. Two and three make five.
Look at the empty spaces. They show the missing part.
The parts can swap places. If ten loses seven, three is left.
I can see five, but the whole is eight. How can I find the hidden part?
The whole is everything together.
A part is one group inside the whole.
A number bond shows the parts that make a whole.
The missing part completes the whole.
Touch one seed for each number. One number, one seed.
We do not need to start at one. We can start at twenty-seven and count on.
Twenty-nine has two tens and nine ones. One more makes thirty.
Look at the number before and after. Which number fits?
Each step backwards takes away one.
Can you show me where to start and what number comes next?
Forwards means the numbers grow by one each step.
Backwards means the numbers go down by one each step.
A ten is a full group of ten counters.
Notice the edge, corner, surface or end. There is no need to answer quickly.
Flat shapes can be drawn on a card. Solid shapes can be held and turned.
First line up the ends. Then place equal cubes with no gaps or overlaps.
Use the tool that matches the question. You may tap, move, point or tell an adult.
Hear the whole prompt. Replay it if you need to. Then choose.
I counted six cubes, but I left gaps. Can you fix my measuring and explain why?
A two-D shape is flat. Trace around its edge.
A three-D shape is solid. It can have flat or curved surfaces.
Length tells us how long something is. Line up the starting ends.
Capacity means how much fits inside a container.
A coin's value is what it is worth, not how big it looks.
Before is earlier. After is later.
Think of something you know. A centimetre is small, a metre is much longer, and one hour is sixty minutes.
Ask what you are measuring first. Then choose a tool that measures that thing.
Start at zero. Each space is one centimetre. The pencil ends at twelve centimetres.
Read both measures with their units. Then choose the symbol that matches the comparison.
Coins can look different and still have the same value. On a clock, the long minute hand tells the minutes.
I put the pencil at the edge of the ruler. What should I line it up with?
A unit tells us what kind of measure the number is counting.
An estimate is a sensible guess. We can improve it by thinking about known measures.
A scale has marks. Read the spaces or labelled marks carefully.
Capacity means how much fits inside a container.
Money value means what the coins are worth altogether.
Quarter past has the minute hand on three. Quarter to has the minute hand on nine.
Touch one whole group for each count. Two, four, six. Five, ten, fifteen.
Four groups of two means four groups, with two in each one.
Three groups of five and five groups of three both show fifteen dots. The rows change, but the total does not.
Share one at a time to make five equal groups. Then reset and make groups with five in each.
Name the number of groups, the amount in each group and the total.
Show each group, say the repeated addition, then tell Pip the total.
Equal groups have the same amount in every group.
An array shows equal rows and equal columns.
To multiply, we find how many there are altogether in equal groups.
To divide, we share equally or find how many equal groups can be made.
An even number makes pairs with none left. An odd number leaves one.
Thirty-four has three tens and four ones.
Add the tens, add the ones, then see whether ten ones can make a new ten.
Four ones and eight ones make twelve ones. Ten of them become one new ten.
Take away two tens. The six ones are still there.
You might count back nine, or take seven then two more.
Can you show me the swap?
The tens tell us how many full groups of ten we have.
The ones are the single cubes.
When ten ones join together, we can swap them for one ten.
Use the bond you know. Check with the frame only if you need it.
The same two parts and whole can tell four connected facts.
Each one becomes one ten, so every number is ten times as large.
Six needs four to reach ten, so sixteen needs four to reach twenty.
Work out the left side, then make the right side the same value.
Show the parts and whole, then explain which part is hidden.
A number bond links two parts to one whole.
A related fact keeps the same number relationship.
Derive means use a known fact to find a new fact.
Equal means both sides have the same value.
Think about the object's size, choose a sensible unit and make an estimate before measuring.
Length, mass, capacity and time measure different things. The unit tells us what each number means.
Add six, four, six and four. The perimeter is twenty centimetres because every outside side is included once.
Fourteen thirty-five is two thirty-five in the afternoon. Jump twenty-five minutes to three o'clock, then twenty-five more.
The checker can turn without changing its angle. Parallel lines keep the same gap; perpendicular lines meet at a right angle.
Name the quantity, unit or property you used, then show the check that supports your answer.
Choose a unit that suits the size and the thing being measured.
Perimeter is a length, so add every outside side once and include a length unit.
Duration is the amount of time from the start to the finish.
A right angle is one quarter of a full turn, even when the corner is rotated.
Parallel lines keep the same gap between them.
Perpendicular lines cross or meet to make a square corner.
Fractions need equal parts.
Ten equal planks make one whole bridge. One plank is one tenth.
The ten tells us the whole has ten equal parts. The three tells us three are shaded.
Count the equal parts you shade, but keep all ten parts in the whole.
One tenth is one step out of ten between zero and one.
Can you show me what the ten means?
A tenth is one equal part when the whole is split into ten.
The denominator tells how many equal parts make the whole.
Multiplication means equal groups.
Eight groups are double four groups.
Double thirty-two to get sixty-four.
One array can show two multiplication and two division facts.
Use a known fact if you need one.
Can you show me the double?
The factors are the numbers being multiplied.
The product is the answer when we multiply.
If you know four eights, you can double it to know eight eights.
Ten tens have the same value as one hundred. The arrangement changes, but the total does not.
Three hundreds, seven tens and four ones make three hundred and seventy-four.
The tens column is empty, but the zero keeps the hundreds and ones in their correct places.
First use standard parts. Then exchange one hundred or one ten and check that the total is unchanged.
The hundreds match, so compare the tens. To find ten less, change the tens value while tracking any exchange.
Show what happens to the tens and hundreds, and explain why the ones stay the same.
A digit's place tells us whether it is worth hundreds, tens or ones.
Partition means split a number into parts that still combine to make the same whole.
In 406, zero shows there are no tens and keeps the 6 in the ones place.
Compare the hundreds first. If they match, compare the tens, then the ones.
Start at zero, zero. Count across on x, then count up on y.
The first number is across. The second number is up. Together they name one point.
Drop a guide line to the x-axis, then across to the y-axis. Say x then y.
Moving right increases x. Moving up increases y.
These two points use the same numbers, but the order changes the position.
I matched one number but forgot the other side of the rectangle. Can you show me the missing corner?
A coordinate gives the across number first and the up number second.
The x-axis runs left to right. Count across on x first.
The y-axis runs up and down. Count up after x.
A translation slides every point the same distance in the same direction.
Before we compare fractions, the wholes must be the same size.
One half and two quarters reach the same point, so they are equivalent.
The parts are still eighths, so we count three eighths and two more eighths.
Fill three full rows and seven more squares to show thirty-seven hundredths.
If one whole is split into more equal parts, each part is smaller.
I added the bottom numbers too. Can you show me why the pieces are still fifths?
Equivalent fractions have different names but the same value.
The denominator tells us the number of equal parts in one whole.
The numerator tells us how many equal parts are counted.
A hundredth is one tiny part when a whole is split into one hundred equal parts.
Area is the inside space. Perimeter is around the edge.
To measure area, cover the space with equal squares.
Four rows of five square units makes twenty square units.
Each square tile counts once.
Area counts inside squares, not the outside edge.
I added the length and width. Why did I miss some squares?
Area is the space covered inside the shape.
Each little square is one square unit.
Perimeter is around the outside, not the inside.
Let's warm up the engine with facts you already know.
Equal rows help us see a multiplication fact all at once.
Break seven rows into five rows and two rows. Forty plus sixteen is fifty-six.
Build it first. Then choose the helpful parts.
Choose the helper fact that keeps equal groups.
Can you teach me how swapping the factors keeps the product?
A factor is one of the numbers in a multiplication fact.
The product is the result made by the factors.
Estimate first, then line up thousands, hundreds, tens and ones.
Each column has a value. We calculate from the ones and record every exchange.
Ten ones are exchanged for one ten. The total stays the same as the counters move to a new column.
Say the value of the column, make any exchange, then record the digit.
Do not swap the digits. Rename a larger place-value unit and keep the total equivalent.
Explain what answer size you expect, then show how addition can undo the subtraction.
Place value tells us whether a digit means ones, tens, hundreds or thousands.
Exchange one ten for ten ones, or one hundred for ten tens, without changing the total.
An estimate is a sensible nearby answer that helps us notice mistakes.
Addition and subtraction are inverse operations because each can undo the other.
Use stated marks and values as evidence. A picture alone may not be drawn to scale.
First read the conventions. Next show the relationship. Finally state the evidence for your answer.
Angles on a straight line total 180 degrees. Graph change is the difference between two correctly read values.
At every station, name the evidence and unit before you submit.
Check equality marks for the polygon and calculate the value of one graph interval.
Give an example, name the convention and explain one mistake the rule prevents.
Regular means every side and every angle matches, not simply that the shape looks balanced.
A reflex angle is the larger turn beyond a straight angle and before a full turn.
Read the labelled values, then work out what each equal interval represents.
Read along the correct row and column, then calculate elapsed time using hours and minutes.
Name the final quantity and its unit before choosing any operation.
A plan shows which result unlocks the next step.
First find the total after the delivery. Then subtract the removed books. Keep each result labelled.
Plan before calculating. Label each answer, then ask whether the final value is sensible.
Words can appear in different relationships. Decide what is known and what is missing.
Explain what each calculation finds, why it comes next and what would make the final answer unreasonable.
A multi-step problem has connected stages. One result may be needed in the next calculation.
An intermediate result is useful working, but it may not answer the question yet.
Choose an operation from how the quantities are connected, not from one clue word.
A reasonable answer is close to an estimate and makes sense in the situation.
Twenty-five out of one hundred can be shown on a hundred grid.
Percent means out of one hundred.
Twenty-five percent, twenty-five hundredths and zero point two five all show the same amount.
Seven percent is seven hundredths, so it is zero point zero seven.
These three cards show the same amount.
I put seven in the tenths column. Why should it be hundredths?
Percent means out of one hundred.
A hundredth is one part when a whole is split into one hundred.
Decimals use place value after the decimal point.
Equivalent fractions need the same whole.
The shaded amount stays the same, even when the pieces get smaller.
Both the shaded parts and total parts doubled, so the value stayed the same.
Whatever factor you use, use it on the top and the bottom.
Equivalent fractions land at the same place on a number line.
Six pieces sounds like more. Can you show me why the value is the same?
Equivalent fractions have the same value.
A factor can scale the numerator and denominator together.
To compare fractions fairly, the wholes must be the same size.
The box hides a number. Use the relationship on both sides to find it.
The letter n can take different number values, but the rule stays the same.
Replace c with five: four times five, then add three, gives twenty-three.
Keep both sides equal. Undo plus two, then undo times three.
Test each pair by checking whether the two values total twelve.
Explain what the equals sign says about the value on each side.
A variable is a symbol whose value can change.
An unknown is the number we need to work out.
A formula is a general calculation rule.
To substitute, put the given number wherever the symbol appears.
Equivalent expressions have the same value.
First decide what the problem is asking.
Strong mathematicians plan, solve and check.
First multiply for the ticket cost. Then subtract the discount.
The order of the steps must match the story.
Two hundred times five is about one thousand.
I used the numbers in order. Why did my answer go wrong?
An operation is the calculation you choose.
An estimate helps you check whether your answer makes sense.
Scaling keeps the same groups.
If one side is multiplied, the matching side must be multiplied too.
Two becomes six by multiplying by three, so five also multiplies by three.
The same scale factor travels to both quantities.
Check whether the relationship stayed the same.
Can you show why the same multiplier belongs on both sides?
A scale factor tells how many times bigger or smaller the matching amount is.
A ratio compares one amount with another amount.
A strong answer needs a reason that matches the maths.
Some claims sound sensible, but mathematicians test them carefully.
Twelve is a multiple of four, but it is not a multiple of eight. That one example shows the claim is false.
Use a common denominator or a visual model, then state the conclusion.
One example can support a claim, but it may not prove an always claim.
If the claim says always, one case where it fails is enough.
To justify is to explain why your answer makes mathematical sense.
A claim is something we can test using maths.
A counterexample breaks an always claim.
To prove means to show why a claim must be true.
To generalise is to explain the rule behind the examples.
Before reading a graph, check the value of each interval.
A data detective reads the whole graph, not just the biggest shape.
Read both values from the scale, then subtract to find the increase.
A sector is a part of the whole. Use the total to find the amount.
Add the values, then share the total equally between the four data points.
The line moved three intervals, but each interval might be worth more than one.
The scale tells us what each interval is worth.
A line graph helps us see change between points.
A pie chart shows how a whole amount is shared into parts.
The mean is the fair-share average.
To interpret data is to explain the story it tells.
Terms can only join if their variable part matches.
Like terms are from the same family. x terms join x terms.
Three x plus five x is eight x. The minus two is a number term, so it stays separate.
The sign belongs to the term. Move them together.
Check the variable part before you combine.
Can you explain why a number term cannot join an x term?
A term is one part of an algebra expression.
Like terms have matching variable parts.
In 4x, the coefficient is 4.
Read the category first, then follow across the row to its value.
A trustworthy graph needs labelled axes, units and a scale with equal steps.
First inspect the scale. Each tick is five, so halfway between ten and fifteen is twelve point five if the graph allows decimals.
A repaired graph uses equal gaps for equal number changes and tells the reader what each axis measures.
A graph claim should point to the data values that support it.
I made the gaps different sizes to fit the data. Why might that mislead the reader?
An axis tells us what the numbers or categories on a graph mean.
A scale uses equal steps so values can be read fairly.
A coordinate gives the x value first, then the y value.
A trend is the overall pattern, not just one point.
An outlier sits away from the main pattern in the data.
Zero is a reference point. Values can extend in both directions from it.
The number farther to the right is greater, even when both numbers are negative.
Negative three is closer to zero and lies to the right of negative eight, so negative three is greater.
Subtracting a number is equivalent to adding its opposite. Subtracting negative six means adding positive six.
First change subtract negative five into add positive five, then move five places right.
Which negative is the operation, which negative belongs to the number, and what equivalent addition does the expression show?
Integers are whole numbers on both sides of zero, including zero.
A negative number lies below zero or to the left of zero on a number line.
Opposite numbers are equally far from zero in different directions.
Absolute value tells us distance from zero, so it is never negative.
Difference can be shown as the distance between two points on a number line.
Two red for every three blue shows a ratio of two to three.
In a ratio table, every part is multiplied by the same scale factor.
Multiply both parts by four: two becomes eight and three becomes twelve.
Find the scale factor, then use it on the matching part.
Two parts and three parts make five parts altogether.
I added four to both numbers. Why did the ratio change?
A ratio compares quantities.
A scale factor multiplies each part by the same amount.
Part-part compares one part with another part.
Look closely. What body part can you see?
A fish, a bird, a frog and a human are all animals.
Where an animal lives is one clue, but it is not the whole answer. A whale is a mammal.
Choose a group, then show the feature you noticed.
Think about the body part you use to notice a sound.
I thought humans were not animals. What can we observe that helps us understand?
An animal is a living thing. Humans are animals too.
Humans, dogs, bats and whales are mammals.
Body parts help animals move, eat and sense their surroundings.
Our senses help us notice light, sound, smell, taste and touch.
Scientists observe carefully before they decide.
A cup is an object. Plastic can be the material. Listen for which kind of word you hear.
Objects can be made from different materials. We check the particular object in front of us.
We change one thing gently and watch what happens. This sample bends, so we describe it as bendy.
Choose one property question. You never need to taste, smell, break or handle a real sample.
The rule is transparent or not transparent. Look for whether you can see clearly through each sample.
I know this is a spoon, but how can I tell what this spoon is made from?
An object is the thing. A spoon is an object.
A material is what the object is made from. This spoon is made from metal.
A property is something we can observe or test about a material.
Transparent means you can see clearly through the material.
An absorbent material soaks up liquid.
A science group uses a clear rule, such as bendy or not bendy.
Scientists look closely before they name things.
Some plants are flowers, some are trees, and some are grasses.
A pine tree keeps green needles, so it is evergreen.
Drag each picture to the group it belongs to.
Evergreen trees keep their green leaves or needles.
I thought plants were only flowers. Can you show me?
A wild plant grows by itself in places like parks or paths.
Evergreen trees stay green through the year.
Notice the sky, rain and wind clues. We are describing today, not guessing the whole season.
Spring, summer, autumn and winter come in a repeating order. Many things can change as the year goes round.
In the first picture the branches have many leaves. Later, fewer leaves remain. We compare what we can see.
Ask: is this evidence about today's weather, a plant changing, or how long it stays light?
Compare the light parts of the day strips. Daylight length does not tell us whether today is sunny or rainy.
I looked for snow only. What other observations could help me?
The year has four seasons: spring, summer, autumn and winter.
Weather tells us what it is like outside now.
Observe means notice carefully before you decide.
Daylight is the part of the day when it is light outside.
A change is something becoming different over time.
Some young animals look like their adults. Some change a lot as they grow.
Offspring grow into adults. The names can change, like chick and hen, or baby and adult.
A need is something an animal must have to stay alive. A want can be nice, but it is not the same as a survival need.
A helpful day has different kinds of food, movement that suits the person, and hygiene routines such as washing hands.
Look for evidence of what the animal needs and how it changes as it grows.
Exercise means moving the body so it works harder. People can exercise in different ways.
Offspring are young animals. A kitten is the offspring of a cat.
An adult animal is fully grown. Many animals change as they grow.
Survival means staying alive. Animals need air, water and food, and many need shelter.
Healthy choices help bodies work well. Bodies can be different and still be healthy.
Exercise can be walking, dancing, wheeling, playing or stretching.
Hygiene includes washing hands, brushing teeth and covering coughs.
Name visible evidence first. A still picture may not show every sign of life.
Ask: is it alive now, was it once alive, or has it never been alive?
Still does not always mean dead. Wood came from a living tree, but the spoon is not alive.
The log gives shelter and the damp place helps the woodlouse avoid drying out.
Check what each organism needs and what each consumer eats.
The arrow points from the food to the living thing that eats it.
A living thing is alive now and needs things such as food, water or light.
A dead thing was alive in the past.
A rock has never been alive.
A habitat provides the conditions a living thing needs.
A food chain shows food energy passing from one living thing to another.
The spoon is the object. Metal is the material.
A good roof material should keep water out.
Plastic works because water does not soak through it.
Look at what the material can do.
A window needs a material you can see through.
I like the paper colour. Why should I test it?
A material is what an object is made from.
Suitable means it works well for the job.
Say what you can see, feel or measure safely. Do not say what is inside unless evidence shows it.
The first root usually grows down. The shoot grows up. Leaves open as the young plant develops.
On day seven the shoot is taller and has two leaves. It is still the same bean plant.
Change one thing so we can use the result as evidence about that condition.
A seed can use stored food to begin growing. Watch what happens to the young plant over more time without light.
Plants need enough water, but too much can stop roots getting the conditions they need.
A seed can stay quiet for a time and then begin to grow.
A bulb stores food and can grow roots and a shoot.
Germinate means a seed starts growing.
A mature plant is a plant that has developed beyond its young stages.
Suitable temperature means the warmth is right for the plant.
We use several observations to decide whether a plant looks healthy.
Look for the effect. The object may start, stop, speed up, slow down, turn or change shape.
A hand must touch the cart, but a magnet can move a magnetic object across a small gap.
Keep the car and release point the same. Change the surface, measure the distance and compare repeated trials.
Predict, test across a small gap, record the result, and use the evidence to group the objects.
Matching poles repel. Different poles attract.
Name the material if known, describe the magnet test and use the result in your conclusion.
A force is a push or pull. We notice it by its effect.
A contact force needs the objects to touch.
Magnetic force can attract or repel across a gap.
When magnets attract, they pull towards each other.
When magnets repel, they push away from each other.
Every magnet has a north pole and a south pole.
The Sun and a switched-on lamp are sources. The Moon and a road sign reflect light.
Light travels from the lamp to the map, reflects from the map and enters the eye.
A mirror gives a clear directed reflection, but many ordinary surfaces also reflect light into our eyes.
Keep two positions the same. Change one distance, measure the shadow and describe the pattern.
A clearer shadow forms when more light is blocked from reaching the screen.
Use shadows, reflected-light observations or adult-approved instruments. Never look directly at the Sun, even through a camera, lens or dark material.
A light source produces light, such as the Sun, a lamp or a flame.
When light reflects, it bounces from a surface and can travel towards our eyes.
An opaque object blocks light and can make a shadow.
A shadow forms where light cannot reach because an object blocks it.
Plants need things like water and light. Their parts help them get what they need.
Every part of a plant has a job.
The roots take in water. The stem carries it upwards.
Read or listen to the job, then match it to the plant part.
Predict what will happen, then run the greenhouse test.
I thought leaves were just pretty. Can you show me their job?
Roots take in water from the soil and anchor the plant.
The stem holds the plant up and carries water to other parts.
Leaves use light to help the plant make food.
Flowers help many plants make seeds.
We can observe colour and grain size directly. We need a planned test for properties such as water absorption.
Scientists can group the same samples in different useful ways if they state and apply one rule consistently.
Change the rock sample, keep the drop amount and waiting time the same, then compare the evidence.
Some remains or traces are buried by sediment. Over a very long time, layers build and harden, and preserved evidence may remain.
Name the different parts you can observe, then decide whether soil is one substance or a mixture.
For each claim, point to the observation, test result or sequence step that supports it.
A property is something we can observe or test, such as grain size, hardness or water absorption.
A rock that absorbs water takes some water into its tiny spaces.
A fossil can be a preserved remain, impression or trace of something that lived long ago.
Sediment can cover remains and build up in layers.
Organic matter includes tiny pieces of decayed plants and animals.
Soil is a mixture, and different soils contain different amounts of its parts.
Each component has a name and a job.
The bulb lights only when there is a complete path around the circuit.
The path goes out from one terminal and returns to the other terminal.
Connect every gap, then test your circuit.
An open switch makes a gap in the path.
I connected one wire. Why is my bulb still dark?
The cell gives the circuit electrical energy.
A complete circuit has an unbroken loop.
A switch can open the path or close it.
Scientists group living things using features they can observe carefully.
One sort might use backbone or no backbone. Another might use flowering or non-flowering plants. The feature must match the group.
At each branch, answer only the question you can check. Follow the yes or no path.
Choose a question that splits the cards. Then choose the next question for the smaller group.
A habitat change can affect food, shelter, water or light. Use the evidence before deciding.
A useful classification feature is something others can observe and check.
Classification means grouping living things by features they share.
A classification key uses questions to guide you to a name or group.
A feature is an observable clue you can use for grouping.
A habitat is the place where a living thing lives and finds what it needs.
Environmental change means surroundings have changed in a way that can affect living things.
Look for the part that vibrates. That movement is the clue.
When the object vibrates, it pushes nearby material back and forth. Those vibrations can reach your ear as sound.
The object vibrates, the air passes the vibration along, and the ear detects it.
Pitch is high or low. Volume is loud or quiet. Change one thing and record what happens.
As the listener moves farther away, the sound usually becomes fainter.
I thought high pitch meant loud. Which observation proves they are different?
A vibration is a quick back-and-forth movement that can make sound.
Sound vibrations travel through a medium to reach your ear.
Pitch tells us whether a sound is high or low.
Volume tells us whether a sound is loud or quiet.
The sound source is where the vibration begins.
Classify what the material does, not whether it feels hard or can be poured.
A pile of grains can pour, but each grain remains a small solid with its own shape.
The water changes state, but it is still water. Follow the arrows for melting, freezing, evaporation and condensation.
Move the temperature in safe steps. Record what you observe before naming the change.
The liquid water changes state at its surface and mixes with the air as water vapour.
Where was the water before the droplets appeared, and what change of state happened?
A material's state can be solid, liquid or gas.
Melting is the change from solid to liquid.
Evaporation happens when liquid changes into gas at its surface.
Condensation is the change from gas to liquid.
Temperature can be measured in degrees Celsius, written with a degree sign and C.
Rotate means spin. Orbit means move around something.
A model helps us explain movement that is too big to see all at once.
Day and night happen because Earth spins.
Earth travels around the Sun on its orbit.
The Moon does not make its own light. It reflects sunlight.
I thought the Sun goes around us every day. Can you show me the model?
An orbit is a path around another object.
Earth rotates. That means it spins.
The Moon reflects light from the Sun.
A force is inferred from what changes in an object's motion or shape.
Arrow direction shows the direction of the force. Compare arrows only within the model's stated scale.
Mass and drop height stay the same. Canopy area changes. The larger area increases air resistance.
Change one factor, keep the comparison fair and use repeated results rather than one dramatic trial.
The mass is the same. Shape changes the air resistance, so the falling motion changes.
A smaller force acts over a greater distance. Show the input, pivot, load and movement trade-off.
Gravity pulls unsupported objects towards the Earth.
Resistance acts in the opposite direction to movement.
Friction can slow movement, provide grip or produce heating.
A mechanism can change force size, direction, speed or distance.
Change one variable, measure the effect and keep important conditions the same.
A stage describes the organism at a time. A process is something happening, such as growth or reproduction.
The adult does not become young again. Reproduction begins a new organism and the pattern can repeat.
A calf and chick share much of the adult body plan. A tadpole and beetle larva change body form more dramatically before adulthood.
Use observations, photographs and dates to separate what was seen from what the model explains.
The adult produces or contributes to new offspring. The adult does not reverse into an egg.
Name the organisms, compare the same feature and say whether your support is an observation, data pattern or life-cycle model.
A life cycle shows the repeated pattern across generations, not one organism going backwards.
Reproduction produces new organisms of the same kind.
Metamorphosis is a major change of body form, such as tadpole to frog or larva to adult insect.
Germination begins when a seed starts growing.
Say what was seen or measured first. Then give the explanation that the evidence supports.
The useful question is not simply what a material is called, but how it behaves in the conditions that matter.
Dissolve the salt in water, filter out the insoluble sand, then recover salt from the solution by evaporation in a controlled model.
Test the property that matters, keep the comparison fair and limit the conclusion to the materials and conditions tested.
The solution's mass and the recovery of sugar after water removal can show that the dissolved substance remains.
Name the property difference, the evidence and one limit on the claim.
A property is evidence about how a material behaves under stated conditions.
In a solution, the solute is still present even when its particles are too small to see.
A separation method works because the substances have a useful difference in property.
A reversible change can be undone, although the recovery may require a process and may not be perfect in a classroom test.
Evidence of new materials can support an irreversible-change explanation.
Scientists classify living things using evidence. Start with features you can observe or describe clearly.
A good branching key question splits the evidence clearly. It should not ask you to guess the name too soon.
We are not sorting by cute, scary or where it lives. We are checking whether the evidence shows a backbone.
Try a question that each organism can answer yes or no. If all cards go the same way, the question does not help yet.
Micro-organisms are living things that are usually too small to see without a microscope. They are not all the same.
I sorted these together because they all live in water. What evidence should I check next?
Classification means grouping living things using evidence about their shared characteristics.
A characteristic is a feature you can observe or describe carefully.
A branching key uses clear yes-or-no questions to guide a classification decision.
A micro-organism is usually too small to see without a microscope. Some are helpful, some are harmful, and many are simply part of ecosystems.
Evidence is what you observed that supports your classification.
We investigate circuits using safe models. A real mains socket is never part of these activities.
When we add suitable cells in series, the bulb may become brighter. The component must be rated for the circuit.
To explain the result, change one thing, measure one thing, and keep the rest the same.
A circuit diagram does not look like the real objects. It uses agreed symbols to show the connections clearly.
The switch opens or closes the path. When the path is closed, suitable components can work.
Use the table and the circuit model together. The best answer names the changed variable and the observed effect.
Could I try this with any battery or a wall socket? Explain the safe science rule.
Voltage helps us compare cells in a circuit model. More cells can make some components brighter or louder, if the components are suitable.
In a series circuit, the components are connected in a single complete loop.
Circuit symbols help scientists and engineers draw circuits clearly.
A component is one part of a circuit with a job to do.
A fair circuit investigation changes one variable at a time.
Members of the same species share many features, but they are not identical.
Individuals live and reproduce. Populations can change across generations.
The plants do not grow deep roots because they try. Some already vary, and inherited helpful variation becomes more common over generations.
Predict first, run the model, then explain what changed in the population.
Fossils give evidence, but gaps in the record mean we should not claim more than the evidence shows.
Use the words variation, inherited, survive, reproduce and generations.
Inheritance is how some characteristics pass from parents to offspring.
Variation means individuals in a population are not all identical.
An adaptation is an inherited feature that gives an advantage in a particular environment.
Evolution is population change across many generations.
Fossils are evidence of organisms that lived long ago.
A population is a group of the same kind living in one area.
Opaque materials block light. Transparent materials let light through.
We draw light rays as straight lines to model the path of light.
Where the object blocks the light, the screen stays dark.
Changing the object's position changes which rays are blocked.
Light travels in straight lines, so the blocked area follows the object's outline.
Could the light just curve around the card? Show me with rays.
Opaque materials block light and can make shadows.
A ray is a model line showing where light travels.
A shadow forms where light is blocked by an object.
Scientists choose the enquiry type that fits the question. Some questions need a fair test; others need observation, classification or pattern seeking.
For this fair test, change one thing, measure one thing and keep the important other things the same.
We change the number of suitable cells, measure brightness on the meter, and keep the bulb, wires and switch the same.
A table keeps results organised. A graph can help reveal a pattern, but only when the graph matches the data.
A scientific conclusion should point back to the evidence. It should not just repeat what we hoped would happen.
My result was surprising, so I will just change everything next time. What would be a better scientific improvement?
A variable is something that can change. In a fair test, we decide what to change, what to measure and what to keep the same.
A fair test changes one important thing so we can see what effect it has.
A prediction says what you think will happen and why.
A conclusion answers the enquiry question using the results.
Reliable results are easier to trust because the method and measurements are careful.
Organisms contain organ systems, organs contain tissues, and tissues are made from cells.
A microscope image provides observational evidence. A model simplifies features so we can identify and explain them.
The membrane forms the cell boundary, the cytoplasm fills much of the cell, and the nucleus contains genetic material and helps control activities.
Both plant and animal cells have a membrane, cytoplasm and genetic material. Plant-cell models also show a cellulose cell wall, chloroplasts and a permanent vacuole.
The cellulose cell wall supports the plant cell. The cell membrane controls movement of substances into and out of the cell.
Which features come from observation, and which features have been simplified to help us reason?
A cell is the fundamental unit of a living organism.
The cell membrane controls what enters and leaves the cell.
The nucleus contains genetic material and helps control cell activities.
Many of the cell's chemical reactions happen in the cytoplasm.
Mitochondria are the site of aerobic respiration, which releases energy for cell processes.
Chloroplasts contain chlorophyll and are the site of photosynthesis.
We can observe light and warmth, then use an energy model to explain the transfers.
Stores say where energy is held. Pathways say how energy is transferred.
Energy is transferred electrically from the chemical store in the battery. Some is transferred by radiation as light and some by heating to the surroundings.
Changing insulation changes how quickly energy is transferred by heating to the surroundings.
Heating is a transfer pathway. Thermal energy store is the store.
I thought the energy vanished. Can you show where it was transferred?
An energy store is a way energy is stored in a system.
A transfer moves energy between stores.
Dissipated energy is spread out to the surroundings.
Forces are pushes or pulls. We will show them with arrows.
A force arrow starts on the object and points in the direction of the force.
The book has weight downward and a normal contact force upward. The forces are balanced, so the book's motion does not change.
If the forward force is larger than the backward force, the resultant force is forwards and the sledge speeds up.
At steady speed in a straight line, the forces are balanced.
I thought balanced meant stopped. Can you explain steady motion?
A force is a push or pull.
Balanced forces are equal and opposite, so the motion does not change.
The resultant force is the overall force on the object.
First sort what we can observe, then we will explain it with particles.
A model helps us explain what is happening, even when particles are too small to see.
Solid particles vibrate in fixed positions. Liquid particles move past each other. Gas particles are far apart and move freely.
When particles gain energy, they move more and may change arrangement.
The particles do not disappear. Their arrangement and movement change.
I thought solid particles were frozen still. Can you show me the vibration?
Particles are tiny pieces that make up substances.
A state tells us whether the substance is solid, liquid or gas.
Melting happens when particles gain energy and move out of fixed positions.
A testable question can be investigated using observations or measurements.
Change one thing, measure one result and keep important conditions the same.
We change the paper towel type, measure the volume of water absorbed and keep the water volume and towel size the same.
A prediction can be supported or not supported by evidence. Either result can teach us something.
In a fair test, the independent variable changes on purpose. Control variables stay the same.
I wrote what I thought would happen. Can you help me use the measurements?
The independent variable is the one thing you change on purpose.
The dependent variable is what you measure or observe.
Control variables are kept the same so the investigation is fair.
